extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The property requires globally(opted_out implies not sent). The transition [t_resume_glitch] on send_attempt with guard (opted_out and ticks >= 1) sets sent := true, which directly contradicts the property when its guard holds.

Reachability of the glitch guard: opted_out becomes true only via [t_optout] (on optout), and nothing ever resets it to false. ticks >= 1 is achieved only via [t_tick] (on tick), which increments ticks from 0 to 1. So firing optout then tick puts the machine in opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=1 — the exact precondition for [t_resume_glitch].

Witness execution (3 events, within the 5-event bound):
1. Initial: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0
2. optout -> [t_optout] (guard true): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0
3. tick -> [t_tick] (guard true): ticks=1 (since ticks<1), sent=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=1
4. send_attempt -> here both [t_send_blocked] (guard opted_out) and [t_resume_glitch] (guard opted_out and ticks>=1) are enabled. With no stated priority, [t_resume_glitch] is a valid enabled transition; firing it gives sent := true. State: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=1.

This reachable state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, violating opted_out implies not sent. Because the verification considers any enabled transition, the presence of an enabled glitch transition that produces the bad state is sufficient to violate the global property.
